topic: Someone finds their parent's draft letter of resignation from decades ago
raw_output:
[story 1]

The brittle paper felt alien beneath my fingertips, yellowed and fragile. It was tucked inside a forgotten box of old tax documents, a relic from a time I barely registered. Unfurling the parchment revealed elegant, looping script—my father’s handwriting. A resignation letter. The words, stark and formal, detailed his departure from a company I never knew existed, signed with a flourish that now seemed mocking. A cold knot tightened in my chest, a familiar, unpleasant pressure building behind my sternum. I crumpled the paper slowly, the sound of the tearing sound amplified in the silence of my apartment. Why keep this? Why leave this behind? It was a petty act of preservation, a stubborn clinging to something that felt fundamentally flawed. I stared at the ink, feeling a surge of bitter resentment towards the man who had signed it, and by extension, towards the legacy he left behind.

[story 2]

I found the letter while clearing out the attic, a dusty, suffocating space where memories went to decompose. It was a folded piece of thick cardstock, smelling faintly of aged paper and cedar. When I read the precise, detached language of the resignation, a sharp, acidic taste flooded my mouth. My jaw clenched so tightly I could feel the strain in my molars. Every line seemed designed to smooth over anything messy, anything imperfect. I tossed it onto the floor, watching it flutter down like a defeated bird. A wave of profound irritation washed over me, a deep-seated urge to erase the very existence of that document, the memory it represented. I didn't want the sentiment; I wanted the emptiness it filled, the sense that some things, once written, could never be truly undone.

[story 3]

The discovery happened quite by accident, tucked into the lining of an old winter coat I hadn't worn in years. When I unfolded the document, the familiar slant of my mother’s cursive hit me like a physical blow. It was a formal statement of leaving, a neat little declaration of severance from some distant obligation. A tremor ran through my hands, making the paper tremble. I didn't look closely at the content; I only registered the sheer act of it—the finality of it. A simmering resentment began to boil beneath my skin, hot and restless. I stood up abruptly, pacing the small room, my breath coming in shallow, quick gasps. This paper was evidence of a life I didn't want to inherit, a neat little historical footnote that just grated on my nerves, confirming everything I already suspected about the foundations of my family.
